name: Tristan
description: Tristan, also known as Tristram, is a legendary Cornish knight and one of the main characters of the Tristan and Iseult story. He is renowned for his skill as a knight, his loyalty to his uncle King Mark of Cornwall, and most famously, his tragic love affair with Iseult (Isolde), the Irish princess betrothed to Mark. Born to Blancheflor and Rivalen, Tristan's early life was marked by hardship and loss, shaping him into a skilled warrior and a melancholic figure. He is often depicted as a master of disguise and deception, using his cunning to navigate treacherous political landscapes and protect his love. Tristan's journey takes him from Cornwall to Ireland and Brittany, encountering giants, dragons, and rival knights along the way. His love for Iseult, ignited by a love potion, becomes the defining aspect of his existence, leading to betrayal, exile, and ultimately, his tragic death. Tristan's story explores themes of forbidden love, duty versus desire, and the destructive power of fate. He stands as a symbol of passionate, yet doomed romance, forever intertwined with the legend of Iseult. His character has been interpreted and adapted throughout history, making him a central figure in Arthurian literature and a lasting symbol of romantic tragedy.
subtitle: The Knight of Love and Loss

keyMyths.title: The Love Potion
keyMyths.description: Tristan and Iseult accidentally drink a love potion intended for Iseult and King Mark, sparking their forbidden love. This event seals their fate, leading to lifelong passion and despair.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Tristan and Iseult legend

keyMyths.title: Slaying the Morholt
keyMyths.description: Tristan defeats the Morholt, an Irish giant who demands tribute from Cornwall, saving his kingdom. However, he is wounded by the Morholt's poisoned blade, leading to his journey to Ireland.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Tristan and Iseult legend

keyMyths.title: Tristan's Death
keyMyths.description: Tristan, wounded and dying, sends for Iseult, asking her to come and heal him. He instructs his wife, Iseult of the White Hands, to signal his ship's return with a white sail if Iseult is aboard, and a black sail if she is not. His jealous wife lies, telling him the sail is black, and Tristan dies of grief before Iseult arrives.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Tristan and Iseult legend

sources.author: Thomas of Britain
sources.description: One of the earliest and most influential versions of the Tristan and Iseult story.
sources.text: One of the earliest and most influential versions of the Tristan and Iseult story.

sources.author: Gottfried von Strassburg
sources.description: A highly influential Middle High German version of the Tristan and Iseult story.
sources.text: A highly influential Middle High German version of the Tristan and Iseult story.

sources.author: Sir Thomas Malory
sources.description: Includes Tristan as a knight of the Round Table and references his love affair with Iseult.
